Topic 1: Main story (Iran war update)

[107:03]Tonight, I actually want to focus on the actual course of the war how the war is being fought

[107:09]And as you know, this is an air and naval campaign

[107:15]So far

[107:16]This is what the fighting looks like.

[107:19]The US and Israel are deployed with their naval and air assets and they are launching

[107:24]air strikes over Iran.

[107:26]They are dropping tons of bombs and they are hitting everything.

[107:29]They are bombing the missile launch platforms, missile production facilities.

[107:34]They're bombing Iran's Navy.

[107:36]They're bombing the regime, the actual leadership of the government and of the military.

[107:45]Retaliating, well, they have basically no other capability other than the missiles.

[107:51]They do not have a modern enough Air Force.

[107:54]They do not have an anti-aircraft system at all.

[107:59]Now they don't have a Navy.

[108:01]They basically are incapable of doing anything other than launching short and medium range

[108:09]Which are interceptable, but if they launch them in a large enough volume and across a

[108:15]broad enough swath of territory, eventually they're counting on the United States to not

[108:21]be able to intercept them.

[108:23]So the U S and Israel are hitting Iran. They say they've hit 1700 targets. It's regime targets,

[108:30]military targets. It's all their assets. Iran retaliates with short range, medium range ballistic

[108:36]missiles against the following targets. They're going after U S bases in all of the Persian

[108:51]They're hitting the US bases in Erbil, which is Iraqi Kurdistan.

[108:55]They're hitting US bases in Jordan.

[108:58]They're also striking the country of Israel, hitting military targets, government targets

[109:04]there.

[109:04]And they're also hitting civilian targets in the Gulf countries as well.

[109:09]So in addition to the US bases in those countries, they're also actually hitting residential infrastructure,

[109:16]hotels, the civilian airport.

[109:19]Now, again, the United States, Israel and the Gulf countries are able to intercept most

[109:25]of these missiles.

[109:26]So Iran has fired, I think in the first 24 hours, they fired 300 missiles, not a lot.

[109:33]And mostly these are being intercepted by US provided weapon systems.

[109:39]So, we've put in place these Patriot missile batteries and THAAD batteries as well as ballistic

[109:45]missile interceptors on the destroyers that are shooting down all the incoming missiles.

[109:51]The goal for Iran is to overwhelm these air defenses, either with large volleys of missiles

[109:58]in a single area or over a sustained period of time.

[110:02]If they can maintain the ability to launch the missiles, they're counting on the United

[110:06]States, Israel and the Gulf to run out of interceptors.

[110:10]So they won't be able to shoot them down.

[110:12]And that has been the course of the war.

[110:20]Here, and to be very precise and specific, we talked about this in the buildup to the

[110:25]war, and it's equally maybe arguably more important now.

[110:29]What Trump laid out on Friday is that this is a regime change war.

[110:34]So the strategic objective of the United States in the conflict, among others, is they are

[110:40]seeking a regime change.

[110:42]That is what they said.

[110:43]In addition to that, Trump said that they are seeking,

[110:47]the US is seeking to destroy Iran's Navy

[110:50]and its ballistic missile capability.

[110:52]So that means obviously sinking all their ships

[110:56]and also not just destroying the platforms

[110:58]that launch the missiles,

[111:00]but also the facilities where the missiles are produced.

[111:04]This is where it gets a little bit tricky

[111:06]because Iran's missile production facilities

[111:09]and their launch platforms are fortified underground.

[111:13]So, just like the complications with the nuclear program and the nuclear complex, the sites

[111:19]at Fordo and Natanz, where the centrifuges are buried under mountains and buried under

[111:23]the ground, it's the same story with the missiles.

[111:26]They have basically built entire cities fortified underneath the ground and just look at a topographical

[111:34]map of Iran.

[111:35]It's all mountains.

[111:41]And this makes it very difficult to destroy these completely and then to verify that the

[111:47]production facilities have been destroyed.

[111:49]And so even though Israel claims that these joint operations have destroyed half of Iran's

[111:55]ballistic missile launch platforms, it's an open ended question whether we will be able

[112:00]to destroy all of them.

[112:03]And that raises some serious questions, but before we get into the missile aspect of the equation, because let's just say succinctly for the sake of argument right now, the two strategic objectives here are regime change and destroying the missile launch capability.

[112:21]We'll talk about regime change first.

[112:24]This is maybe the most controversial operative word in this entire conflict and maybe in

[112:31]the second Trump administration.

[112:33]Because historically what a regime change means is that you kill the leader and you replace

[112:39]them with a different leader and that new leader brings all his people in and you just

[112:44]get a new country and you realign the country.

[112:57]we killed them, we killed all the remnant of the bathist force, and others of them were

[113:03]And now they have a democratic regime as opposed to a bathist regime.

[113:08]And the same thing happened in Syria.

[113:10]We back proxies in Syria. We dropped tons of ordinance. We overthrew the bathist Assad regime and in its place is now a Sunni

[113:19]Theocracy effectively

[113:21]We actually don't know the character of the new regime

[113:23]But this new character Ahmed al-Sharah formerly known as al-Julani

[113:34]from the fascist pan-Arab socialist ideology of baptism and towards something like, I'm

[113:43]not even quite sure what he's going for. They're maintaining a liberal structure, relatively

[113:48]liberal structure, but it is going to be a Muslim religious regime.

[113:53]All that being said, it's a completely new governing ideology.

[113:57]It's a new leader and it's a new security apparatus.

[114:00]That is historically what regime change means in the Middle East.

[114:04]But with Trump, it's a little bit different because like we talked about with Venezuela,

[114:10]when Trump went into Venezuela in January, he did not actually have a real regime change.

[114:20]Trump kidnapped Nicholas Maduro, who was the head of state and head of government, the

[114:24]president of Venezuela.

[114:26]And so people would say that appears to be a regime change operation because the head

[114:31]of state has been removed from power.

[114:35]However, you could argue this is more of like a hybrid approach.

[114:39]Even though Nicholas Maduro is no longer there, his vice president succeeded him and became

[114:44]president.

[114:45]Everybody else remained. It's the same cabinet. It's the same government, same defense minister,

[114:51]same intelligence minister, same interior minister, same, the vice president is now the

[114:56]president, same army, same intelligence operation, same security apparatus. And they still ostensibly

[115:03]are a socialist Chavista regime. So is it safe to say that that's regime change? Not quite.

[115:14]And it's using decapitation as a menu item, as a tool, maybe college smart power for persuasion.

[115:24]In other words, rather than go to war against Venezuela in a conventional sense, to destroy

[115:30]their military and put everybody on trial and find new leaders and create a democracy

[115:36]Instead, we are picking off individuals in the government with these very surgical operations,

[115:44]I guess it's just the one, in the hopes that the successor will be more compliant to Washington.

[115:52]And so this is seen as a cost-effective low-intensity way to reorient the posture of a hostile or

[115:59]revisionist country like Venezuela.

[116:06]So, Trump laid out the case on Friday for a regime change.

[116:09]He said, this is a terrorist regime.

[116:12]They've been fighting us for years.

[116:13]They've killed Americans in Iraq and in Beirut and elsewhere.

[116:40]tend to imply that there will be a new regime.

[116:43]that the people of Iran are going to take power

[116:47]and they're going to create some kind of popular government,

[116:51]like a democracy or a liberal regime,

[116:53]but it will not be the Islamic Revolutionary Guard.

[116:57]It will not be the Islamic Revolutionary Regime.

[117:00]It will be something else.

[117:01]So that's the message we got on Friday and that's what it sounded like was full-fledged regime change

[117:07]We're gonna take care of their big guns the cannons the Navy the missiles and once we're done

[117:20]It seems though that Trump has been walking that back

[117:24]And now they're talking about a different kind of regime change where Trump is talking

[117:30]about there may be candidates from within the existing government that will succeed

[117:35]the government that has been destroyed.

[117:37]So after the Ayatollah was assassinated in the initial strikes on Saturday, a council

[117:43]has been convened to operate the government and try to maintain some continuity.

[117:53]Trump says that every candidate he had in mind to lead the country has been killed.

[117:58]He says, and we're almost done killing all the leadership.

[118:22]seeing that Iran is totally defenseless

[118:31]is gonna seize power and they will initiate negotiations

[118:34]and give the United States what they want.

[118:40]That sounds like if that is coming from inside the regime,

[118:45]then it's not a real regime change.

[118:55]There's a lot of opposition to the regime in Iran, but they're not cohesive and they're
